parentQuestion: A tissue consists of groups of similar cells that have a common function.

scaffolds.q: What is the organizational hierarchy of cells, tissues, and organs?
scaffolds.explain: The body is organized hierarchically: CELLS → TISSUES → ORGANS. Each level builds on the one below it.

wrongExplains.0: This is the reverse order; cells are smaller than tissues.
wrongExplains.2: Cells come before tissues in the hierarchy.

scaffolds.q: Where does tissue fit in the organizational hierarchy?
scaffolds.explain: A TISSUE is the level BETWEEN cells and organs. Cells group together to form tissues.

wrongExplains.0: Tissues are above cells in complexity.
wrongExplains.2: Organs are above tissues in complexity.
wrongExplains.3: Tissues are much larger and more complex than molecules.

scaffolds.q: What type of cells make up a tissue?
options: Similar cells — cells of the same type
scaffolds.explain: Tissues are made of SIMILAR cells — cells of the same type. Muscle tissue = muscle cells. Nerve tissue = nerve cells.

wrongExplains.0: Tissues contain similar cells, not different types.
wrongExplains.2: Many tissues exist; they're not all blood cells.
wrongExplains.3: Many tissues exist; they're not all nerve cells.

scaffolds.q: What do the cells in a tissue do together?
options: Work together toward a common function
scaffolds.explain: The cells in a tissue work together toward a COMMON FUNCTION. Muscle tissue contracts. Nerve tissue transmits signals.

wrongExplains.0: Tissue cells share a common function.
wrongExplains.3: Tissue cells work together, not independently.

scaffolds.q: What are the four primary tissue types?
scaffolds.explain: The four PRIMARY TISSUE TYPES are: EPITHELIAL, CONNECTIVE, MUSCLE, and NERVOUS. Every tissue fits into one of these categories.

wrongExplains.0: These are tissue examples, not the primary categories.
wrongExplains.2: These are organs, not tissue types.
wrongExplains.3: These are colors, not tissue classifications.

scaffolds.explain: EPITHELIAL TISSUE covers and LINES SURFACES — skin surface, gut lining, blood vessel lining — all epithelium.

wrongExplains.0: Muscle tissue contracts; epithelium covers surfaces.
wrongExplains.2: Adipose (connective) tissue stores fat; epithelium covers.
wrongExplains.3: Nervous tissue transmits signals; epithelium covers.

scaffolds.q: What is the definition of a tissue?
scaffolds.explain: Definition: TISSUE = SIMILAR CELLS + COMMON FUNCTION. This is the precise definition of a tissue.

wrongExplains.0: A tissue is multiple cells, not a single cell.
wrongExplains.2: An organ is made of multiple tissues.
wrongExplains.3: Tissues are made of many molecules and cells.

scaffolds.q: Is this statement correct: 'A tissue consists of groups of similar cells that have a common function'?
options: No, tissues have different cell types
scaffolds.explain: Yes, this correctly defines tissue: GROUPS OF SIMILAR CELLS with a COMMON FUNCTION. Both parts of the definition are accurate.

wrongExplains.0: Tissues do have similar cell types.
wrongExplains.3: Tissues and organs are different organizational levels.

scaffolds.q: The statement 'A tissue consists of groups of similar cells that have a common function' is ___?
options: True — this is the standard definition of tissue
scaffolds.explain: The answer is TRUE. This is the STANDARD DEFINITION of tissue: Similar cells working together for a common function.

wrongExplains.0: Tissues contain similar cells, not diverse cells.
wrongExplains.2: Tissues have specific functions (contraction, covering, etc.).
